channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Scientific Quest for Perfect World Cup Pitch
item.description: Every match must be played on natural grass that gives players as consistent a surface as possible, no matter the venue. Cue the years of sod studies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: What NASA Needs to Stay on Track for the Moon
item.description: The agency gave a rosy update on Artemis III, a test flight for its goal to return humans to the moon, but experts say the timeline is ambitious.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of the Blue Origin spacecraft on the moon.

item.title: They Bought a Famous Puzzle in Cryptography. Now They’re Opening It Up.
item.description: A San Francisco company paid nearly $1 million for the solution to an unsolved code in Kryptos, a sculpture on the C.I.A. grounds. Soon it will become an online challenge.
item.dc:creator: John Schwartz

item.title: NASA Leader Responds to Criticism Over All-Male Artemis III Crew
item.description: NASA’s missions these days rarely feature all-male crews. Jared Isaacman, its administrator, said women play prominent roles throughout the space agency.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: From left, the crew of the Artemis III: Randy Bresnik, Luca Parmitano, Frank Rubio and Andre Douglas.

item.title: SpaceX’s Unlikely Journey From Far-Out Idea to $2 Trillion Juggernaut
item.description: Elon Musk said he had initially given SpaceX less than a 10 percent chance of succeeding. His rocket company has come a long way.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac, Kenneth Chang and Kirsten Grind
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: A SpaceX ad on the Nasdaq MarketSite tower in Times Square. The company, which Elon Musk founded in 2002, began trading on the stock market on Friday.

item.title: Neil Shubin on Trusted Science in a ‘Deeply Partisan Age’
item.description: An eminent fossil hunter takes the reins at the National Academy of Sciences in a turbulent moment for American researchers.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:credit: Christopher Michel/Contour RA by Getty Images
item.media:description: Neil Shubin, a decorated paleontologist, will lead the academy for five years.

item.title: Scientists Measure Earth’s Vast Underground Fungal Webs
item.description: With machine learning and a high-resolution imaging robot, scientists measured and mapped the extent of Earth’s carbon circulatory system.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: 26-Year Mystery of a Skeleton in a Tent Ends With DNA Identification
item.description: Remains of the man, Joseph Louis Serrao Jr., discovered in a remote area in Olympic National Park in 2000, were identified using forensic genealogy.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.media:description: Olympic National Park in Washington State.

item.title: She Used A.I. to Create Better Forecasts for Extreme Weather. Then Her Funding Was Cut.
item.description: Amy McGovern was the director of an A.I. institute dedicated to weather forecasting — until the National Science Foundation ended its funding.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Brett Deering for The New York Times

item.title: The Secrets of a Soccer-Turf Master
item.description: John Sorochan, a turf scientist at the University of Tennessee, has led the yearslong, multimillion-dollar effort to develop perfect playing fields for the 2026 World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: Can the Artemis III Mission Go on as Planned?
item.description: NASA has chosen four astronauts for the Artemis III mission, but there has been a major setback: the destruction of a Blue Origin rocket and its only launchpad. Our science reporter Katrina Miller describes what this event might mean for the U.S. goal of landing on the moon by 2028.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller, Melanie Bencosme, Joey Sendaydiego, Lauren Pruitt and Kenneth Chang

item.title: Scientists Race to Test Treatments as Ebola Outbreak Widens
item.description: Trials are beginning on several drugs that have shown promise in preliminary studies against the virus that is causing the current outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer and Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A health worker in the town of Mongbwalu, Democratic Republic of Congo, collecting medication for patients suspected of having Bundibugyo virus last month.

item.title: Renewable Groups Ask Courts to End Pentagon’s ‘Total Halt’ of Wind Power
item.description: More than 100 planned wind farms in 21 states are now stalled indefinitely as the Pentagon delays military reviews once seen as routine.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times
item.media:description: The freeze affects 41 wind projects in Texas, which produces more wind power than any other state in the U.S., as well as projects in Colorado, Illinois, Iowa, Kansas, New Mexico, Oregon and Washington.

item.title: Alan Hale, Sky Watcher Who Created a Comet Sensation, Dies at 68
item.description: In 1995, he and Thomas Bopp spotted, from different states, the same mysterious object in the sky. What turned out to be a comet was named after them: Hale-Bopp.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Alan Hale in 1997 with the telescope he had used a couple of years earlier to find the comet that would be known as Hale-Bopp.

item.title: Cleve Moler, Who Unlocked the Power of Computing for Millions, Dies at 86
item.description: He built interfaces that allowed engineers, scientists and everyday people to solve difficult problems without having to write the underlying code.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: NOAA Issues El Nino Advisory
item.description: The global weather pattern threatens to worsen floods and heat waves already intensifying due to climate change. But it may also mean fewer hurricanes.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: Drought-stressed wheat in a field in Kansas last month.

item.title: The Researcher Who Didn’t Want to Know
item.description: Her decades of work on Huntington’s disease helped lead to the creation of a genetic test for the devastating condition. Why didn’t she take it herself?
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Jackie Molloy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nancy Wexler a lifelong researcher of Huntington’s disease, who has the condition herself, in her Manhattan apartment.

item.title: A Newer Approach to Editing Embryos Ignites Debate
item.description: Fertility specialists, biotech companies and ethicists are divided over whether progress in early gene editing would wipe out diseases or trigger a rush toward enhancement.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:description: A colored light micrograph of a human embryo 48 to 72 hours after in vitro fertilization. “Human embryos become living human persons,” one bioethicist said. “The research is being done on future persons without their consent.”

item.title: What Does ‘Triggered’ Really Mean?
item.description: This popular term is often misused, experts say, which may cause more harm than good.
item.dc:creator: Melinda Wenner Moyer

item.title: Democrats Once Vowed to Stop Oil and Gas. Now They’re Not So Sure.
item.description: As the midterm elections approach, many leading Democrats are rethinking their approach to climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:description: A talk on rising energy costs on Capitol Hill hosted by Democrats, from left, Sen. Martin Heinrich of New Mexico, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island, in March.

item.title: Nearly Everyone, Everywhere, Veers Left When Walking
item.description: Researchers are at a loss for why people across cultures and ages, regardless of their dominant hand, have a natural bias toward wandering in a counterclockwise direction.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer

item.title: NASA Crew-12 Commander Captures Snaky Southern Lights From Space Station
item.description: The footage of the southern lights was shared on Sunday by Jessica Meir, commander of NASA’s Crew-12 mission.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz

item.title: Indonesia Landslides Devastated Endangered Orangutans, Study Finds
item.description: More than 5 percent of the species is estimated to have been lost when a climate-fueled storm unleashed torrents of water, mud and debris.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn

item.title: Bees Are Swarming Earlier: What to Do if You’re Attacked
item.description: Experts discuss the threat and how to escape aggressive bees. Hint: Don’t jump in water.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.media:description: A swarm of bees landed on the protective netting behind home plate during a baseball game in Phoenix in April 2024.

item.title: Meet the NASA Astronauts in the Artemis III 4-Person Crew
item.description: NASA’s next mission in its effort to return humans to the moon will be led by an all-male crew. Three of them have extensive experience in space, while the other was a backup on Artemis II.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller, K. R. Callaway and Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: The crew of Artemis III, from left: Commander Randy Bresnik; pilot Luca Parmitano of the European Space Agency; mission specialist Frank Rubio; and mission specialist Andre Douglas.

item.title: Can NASA Really Land Astronauts on the Moon by 2028?
item.description: Experts have been hopeful, but say the agency’s lunar aspirations are largely at the whims of two billionaires, Elon Musk and Jeff Bezos.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: An artist’s concept released by Blue Origin of its large, crew-carrying Mark 2 moon lander. Neither Blue Origin nor SpaceX, contracted by NASA, have completed development of a lunar lander.

item.title: A Scientific Achievement That’s Totally Random
item.description: Encryption systems rely on “random” numbers, but conventional computers can’t generate them perfectly. New research shows that quantum physics can.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan

item.title: NASA’s moon-base plans are off to a slow start.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: From left, Carlos García-Galán, NASA’s moon-base program executive; Jared Isaacman, the NASA administrator; and Lori Glaze, exploration systems development mission administrator, discussing a moon base last month at NASA headquarters.

item.title: How Will the Artemis III Astronauts Train?
item.description: The Artemis III astronauts who were announced today will have had less mission training time than their Artemis II counterparts.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.media:description: NASA crew members practiced emergency rescue drills during simulated lunar surface operations at NASA’s Neutral Buoyancy Laboratory at the Johnson Space Center in Houston, in preparation for the Artemis III mission.

item.title: The Artemis II mission transfixed the nation.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: Astronaut Victor Glover during the flyby of the moon during the Artemis II mission in April.

item.title: Artemis Astronauts to Wear Prada Spacesuits
item.description: Prada and Axiom Space unveiled the bodysuit that will go to the moon.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lorenzo Bertelli, right, Prada’s chief marketing officer and head of sustainability, onstage with Axiom Space officials to unveil the new Liquid Cooling and Ventilation Garment at Prada’s store in the SoHo neighborhood of Manhattan.

item.title: A Surprising Find in Ancient Squirrel Poop: Woolly Mammoth Meat
item.description: In a new study, fossilized droppings suggested that ancient ground squirrels ate the meat of much larger animals, including mammoths, bison and saber-toothed cats.
item.dc:creator: Kate Golembiewski

item.title: Why is NASA going back to the moon?
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: A team at the NASA Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Ala., was at work simulating the lunar lighting environment in preparation for the Artemis III mission.

item.title: Remember Apollo 9? No? Have a Look at Artemis III.
item.description: Almost forgotten now, the mission circled the Earth for 10 days, performing a series of tests with the lunar lander. What it lacked in glory it made up for in technological contributions.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: Who gets to be a NASA astronaut?
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.media:description: A young space enthusiast at the Artemis II mission rollout at the Kennedy Space Center in Florida in January.

item.title: What Is Artemis III? What to Know About NASA’s Latest Space Mission.
item.description: Artemis III is the third in a series of missions that gets humans closer to returning to the surface of the moon.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: Rolling out a section of NASA’s Space Launch System for the Artemis III mission in New Orleans in April.

item.title: How Does the Blue Origin Rocket Explosion affect NASA’s Moon Plans?
item.description: NASA’s Artemis program has little room for error if it is to land astronauts on the moon by the end of 2028, but Blue Origin could be out of commission for at least a year.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: Blue Origin’s New Glenn rocket exploded on its launchpad during a test on May 28.

item.title: What is the Orion spacecraft?
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mockup of the Orion spacecraft where the Artemis II astronauts trained for their mission at the Johnson Space Center in Houston.

item.title: NASA Will Announce New Artemis III Astronauts and an Update on Its Moon Program
item.description: The agency will announce the crew of Artemis III on Tuesday. But will the mission be ready to fly in 2027?
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller

item.title: As Screwworm Cases Mount, U.S. Officials Ramp Up Response
item.description: The parasitic, flesh-eating fly has now been confirmed in cows, goats and dogs.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway and Emily Anthes
item.media:description: The New World screwworm fly. It lays eggs in the open wounds or orifices of warm-blooded animals; the resulting larvae burrow into and expand the wound, consuming the animal’s tissue.

item.title: Scientist Charged With Conspiring to Smuggle Mpox Virus Into U.S.
item.description: The virologist was stopped at the Detroit airport after working in Congo during an mpox epidemic. His lawyer said the material was for research.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Victoire Douniama for The New York Times
item.media:description: A vial to be sent for mpox testing in the Democratic Republic of Congo in 2024.

item.title: They Spent Years on a Math Problem. Then They Were Scooped by A.I.
item.description: Artificial intelligence is mastering the kinds of projects that have long helped to build the careers of young mathematicians. What does that mean for their future?
item.dc:creator: Gregory Barber
